nodes_flat.title: Safety, Risk Assessment, Emergency Procedures Scope
nodes_flat.topics: Child, staff safety; Risk assessment purpose; Termly emergency drills; Annual risk assessment; Change assessment; H&S Officers: identify hazards; H&S Officers: review controls; H&S Officers: add controls; H&S Officers: assess risks; External Technician: identify hazards; External Technician: detect harm; External Technician: evaluate risks; External Technician: record findings; External Technician: annual review; Emergency plan preparation; Emergency plan validation; Emergency plan practice
nodes_flat.content:
The safety of the children and staff at Imagine Montessori School is paramount. Therefore, the risk assessment ensures the safety of everyone. There is an emergency drill carried out every term. A risk assessment is made annually or when there is a significant change or new risk to consider.

**The Health and Safety Officers:**

*   Identify the hazard, record significant findings and assess the risks
*   Review the existing control measures
*   Where necessary, provide additional control measures
*   Carry out risk assessments

**The External Technician in Emergency Risk Assessment is responsible for:**

*   Identification of hazards
*   Detect who might be harmed and how
*   Evaluate the risks, deciding whether existing controls are adequate, or the school needs to do more
*   Record findings
*   Review and revise annually

These responsible agents collaborate to prepare an emergency plan in accordance with the risks identified. The emergency plan is validated with the Leadership Team and explained and practised with the whole Imagine Team.

nodes_flat.title: Fire Emergency Policy Key Terms
nodes_flat.topics: Fire Emergency Policy; Risk Assessment; Health Safety Officers; External Risk Technician; Emergency Plan; Fire Assembly Point; Contractor Hazard Checklist
nodes_flat.content:
Fire and Emergency Policy: The policy established to ensure the safety of individuals in the building in the event of a fire or other emergency.

Risk Assessment: The process of identifying and evaluating risks to ensure a safe environment.

Health and Safety Officers: Personnel responsible for identifying hazards, implementing control measures, and overseeing safety procedures.

External Technician in Emergency Risk Assessment: An external specialist responsible for evaluating emergency risks and providing expert guidance.

Emergency Plan: A document outlining the procedures to be followed during an emergency to ensure the safety of everyone on the premises.

Fire Assembly Point: A designated area where individuals must gather after evacuating the building.

Contractor Hazard Exchange Form/Checklist: A tool used to assess and mitigate specific risks associated with contractors working on the premises.

nodes_flat.title: Policy statement and safety principles
nodes_flat.topics: Safety commitment; Risk assessments; Hazard mitigation; Emergency drills; Staff training; Emergency response; Evacuation information; Evacuation procedures; Evacuation routes; Emergency equipment; Equipment inspection; Safe emergency environment
nodes_flat.content:
Imagine Montessori School is committed to ensuring the safety of all children, staff, and visitors on campus. This commitment is reflected in the implementation of policies and practices that:

1.  Ensure continuous risk assessments to identify hazards and mitigate them effectively.
2.  Promote regular drills and staff training to guarantee swift and organised responses in case of emergencies.
3.  Provide clear information and evacuation procedures to all parties involved, including families, visitors, and contractors.
4.  Maintain accessible evacuation routes and ensure emergency equipment is in optimal condition through regular inspections.

These principles are designed to ensure a safe and orderly environment during any emergency.

nodes_flat.topics: Visitor registration; Sign in/out; Lanyard requirement; Host escort visitor; Contractor types; Recurrent contractor protocol; Fire safety training; Evacuation procedures; Assembly points; Firefighting equipment; Non-recurrent contractor brief; Emergency exits; Join evacuation team
nodes_flat.content:
**Visitor Registration**

All visitors and contractors must report to Reception, signing in upon arrival and signing out before leaving the premises. Once signed in, they will receive a green lanyard, which must be worn throughout their visit. During a fire evacuation, the staff member hosting the visitor is responsible for ensuring they are escorted to the designated fire assembly point.

**Contractors Safety Procedures**

For contractors, there is a distinction between recurrent and non-recurrent roles:

*   **Recurrent contractors (e.g., cleaning or kitchen staff)** have their adapted Fire and Emergency Protocol, and receive basic fire safety training to ensure they are prepared for emergencies, even outside regular school hours. They are informed of the action to be taken on hearing the fire alarm or discovering a fire, the evacuation procedures including means of escape, location of the fire assembly points and name of the person in charge of evacuation procedures, and the location of firefighting equipment and fire alarm call points in relation to the area of their work.
*   **Non-recurrent contractors** receive a brief summary of emergency procedures upon arrival, including the location of emergency exits and the fire assembly point. In the event of an evacuation, they are instructed to join the nearest evacuation team and follow their guidance to ensure a safe exit.

This approach ensures all individuals on the premises are informed and capable of responding appropriately in an emergency.

nodes_flat.title: Health and Safety Scope and Responsibilities
nodes_flat.topics: Children's activity engagement; Health safety support; Children risk management; Risk assessment proportionality; Staff safety training; Employer safety policy; Occupational Risks Law; Employer risk assessment; Risk management measures; Prevention Services Regulation; Delegating safety tasks; Employer ultimate responsibility; Employer documentation monitoring; Audit trail; Shared safety responsibility; Policy definition; Workplace safety; Facilities oversight; Safety implementation; Head Teachers' role; Administration's role; Collaborative safety structure
nodes_flat.content:
Children should have the opportunity to engage in a broad variety of activities. Health and safety measures should support this goal, ensuring their well-being without hindering their participation. It is crucial for children to learn to recognise and manage risks as part of their everyday life. When assessing and managing risks, common sense should be applied, and health and safety procedures should always be proportionate to the risks associated with each activity. Staff should receive appropriate training to ensure they can protect themselves and the children, and effectively manage any potential risks.

**Responsibility of the school**

The employer is required to have a health and safety policy in place and ensure the necessary measures are implemented to enforce it. This is regulated under the Law 31/1995 on the *Prevention of Occupational Risks*. Employers are obligated to assess the risks associated with all activities, adopt measures to manage those risks, and communicate these measures to their employees. Furthermore, the organisation of preventive activities is governed by the Regulation of Prevention Services (*Royal Decree 39/1997*).

In practice, employers may delegate specific health and safety tasks to designated individuals. However, ultimate responsibility always remains with the employer, regardless of who carries out the tasks.

It is therefore essential for the employer to maintain a clear system of documentation and monitoring to ensure there is an audit trail. This should specify who is responsible for each task and confirm that these tasks are being performed appropriately.

**Responsibility of the employees**

At Imagine Montessori School, responsibility for health and safety is shared across different roles. To manage health and safety effectively, the following responsibilities are defined:

*   **Policy Definition and Oversight:** Specific areas are assigned to designated individuals, such as the Responsibility for Workplace Safety (HR Responsible) and oversight of Buildings and Facilities (Facility Manager).
*   **Implementation:** The practical application of health and safety measures is managed by the Head Teachers and the Head of Administration within their respective domains.

This collaborative structure ensures that all areas of the school’s operations maintain the highest standards of safety and compliance.

nodes_flat.title: General Policy Statement and Duties
nodes_flat.topics: Commitment to safety; Delegation of duties; Building safety checks; Entrance/exit checks; Emergency exit checks; Climbing equipment safety; Garden safety; Lighting checks; Staff awareness
nodes_flat.content:
**A. A general statement of policy**

Imagine Montessori is committed to providing a safe and healthy work environment for all its employees, children and visitors.

**B. Delegation of duties as allocated tasks**

*   The general safety of the building, indoors and outdoors, is checked daily by the Maintenance Team. This includes checking the general condition of the building, e.g. fallen tiles. In finding any damage, the Facilities Manager of the school will be notified immediately. The area will be made safe. If there is any broken glass, litter, cans, ring pulls as well as animal faeces all should be cleared.
*   Check all entrances and exits are clear and in working order.
*   Unlock and check all emergency exits, garden fences and gates, locks and bolts on doors.
*   Check that all climbing equipment is safe. Check the garden for fungi and unwanted dangerous plants.
*   Check that all emergency lighting is in working order, that light switches are safe, and that all areas are adequately lit.
*   Other duties all members of staff are expected to be aware of and uphold.

**

nodes_flat.title: Monitoring, Review, and Training
nodes_flat.topics: Annual monitoring and review; Equipment maintenance; Electrical and gas safety; Fault logging and repair; Hygiene standards; Staff induction; Risk assessment training; Evacuation procedures; First-aid training; Illness awareness; Maintenance staff training; Staff meeting topics
nodes_flat.content:
C. Arrangements made to put in place, monitor and review measures necessary to reach satisfactory health and safety standards:**

All arrangements are monitored and reviewed annually. All electrical and gas equipment is to be maintained and subject to periodical inspections. The service histories should be recorded in a logbook.

Faults and repairs are to be logged. For example, a piece of equipment that is damaged should be labelled and removed until it can be either repaired or discarded. Strict hygiene standards are set and maintained.

**D. Training of staff in health and safety including competence in risk assessment**

All members of staff should have the school Health and Safety procedures clearly explained to them as part of the induction process and are given the Health and Safety policies in their induction pack. This includes the school hygiene regime. As part of a rolling programme, staff are trained in Health and Safety including competence in risk assessment.

All new staff should be trained in the evacuation procedures in the event of a fire or other emergency. All members of staff should have first-aid training (see first-aid policy). All staff are to be kept aware of signs of illness such as meningitis, hepatitis B, AIDS. All maintenance staff must know where to turn the water and electricity supplies off and must be trained to use electrical equipment correctly and safely. Use regular staff meetings to focus on different health and safety issues such as keeping up to date on tetanus vaccinations.

**

**F. Selecting and controlling contractors**

The school will use these indicators to select and control contractors: past performance, work undertaken in other schools, membership of trade bodies, accreditation of trade bodies, general health and safety awareness, organisation and arrangements for health and safety and commitment to recognised codes of practice.

**H. School Security**

This is covered in the check of the inside and external building by the Maintenance Team. The main door is always locked. The doors that are used to access the outside areas are only opened when such areas are under supervision. At the start and end of the school day, two doors are used for children to arrive or depart, and both are continuously supervised by Imagine’s staff. All other doors remain secure and are not used during these times.

**

nodes_flat.title: First Aid, Accidents, Medical, Hygiene
nodes_flat.topics: First-aider qualification; Annual training; First aider ratio; Minor accident referral; Medical emergency response; Trained first-aiders only; Multiple first-aiders; Biannual training; Equipment location; Accident reporting; Parent notification; Bus service notification; Report storage; Risk analysis; Mitigation actions; Serious accident protocol; Head teacher notification; Site safety; Accident investigation; Public involvement; Staff hazard awareness; Staff training; First aid kit access; Pupil medical conditions; Staff awareness; Medication administration; Medication record; Medication storage; Parental consent; Injury treatment levels; Emergency calls; Hygiene procedures; Spillage PPE; Containment; Spillage cleanup steps; Waste disposal
nodes_flat.content:
All Imagine’s staff have qualified first-aider status and are trained on an annual recurrent basis. Therefore, we have well above the required ratio of first aiders to pupils. For minor accidents, they will refer any accidents or any other medical conditions to the designated member of staff: Secretary. For medical emergencies, the person who finds the child/adult will deal with it immediately. Only trained first aiders administer first aid.

There is always more than one designated first-aider on the site of the school. All first aiders attend training courses biannually. First aid equipment is in the designated first aid cupboard, which is kept in the admin staff room and bathroom.

All accidents and other medical emergencies should be recorded appropriately in an accident report form. Parents /guardians should be notified and asked to sign the record sheet and a record kept. In the case of students using our bus service, parents will be notified by Phidias and we expect an acknowledgment. The accident report will be kept both on paper and in digital format at the Secretary.

Based on the information from accident reports, risk analyses are periodically conducted using multivariable analysis to implement mitigation actions for recurring potential risks.

If the accident is serious, the head teacher should be made aware and immediate action taken to ensure the location of the accident is still safe to use. Necessary action should be taken and where possible details recorded for an accident investigation. If members of the public are involved, names and addresses should be taken (including any witnesses).

Members of staff should be made aware of any hazards in the subjects that they teach or the activities that they supervise and should have appropriate training and access to first aid kits.

Pupils with medical conditions, such as epilepsy, asthma or severe allergies should be identified. All staff should be aware of their conditions and alert to the need for prompt action. The Secretary administers medication and is witnessed by another member of staff. A written record is kept and signed by the two members of staff. Medication is kept in the office or the refrigerator in Reception. A signed consent form is needed from the parent /guardian before prescribed medication is administered.

Staff should be aware of what levels of injury can be treated on-site and what levels automatically trigger an emergency ambulance call or visit to the hospital.

There are hygiene procedures in place in case of spillage of blood or body fluid: Employees must wear disposable gloves and aprons when cleaning any bodily fluids (spillages of blood, vomit, urine and excreta). Other persons should be kept away from the contamination until it is effectively dealt with.

The following action should be taken in the event of spillages:

*   Soak up the spillages using paper towels
*   Clean the area with hot water containing soap or detergent
*   Clean the area with a bleach or disinfectant solution - use as directed by the manufacturer.
*   'Solids’ should be carefully flushed down the toilet. Dressings, gloves, and aprons should be carefully disposed of in sealed plastic bags as clinical waste.

nodes_flat.title: Wasp Sting Protocol for Students
nodes_flat.topics: Initial assessment; Student safety; Check for wasps; Sting count; Allergy check; Symptom assessment; Mild symptoms; Emergency symptoms; Remove sting; Wash sting area; Cold compress; Elevate area; Observe student; Anaphylaxis symptoms; Call 112; Administer adrenaline; EpiPen use; Position child; Do not leave; Notify family; Record incident; Monitor student; Check premises; Staff awareness; First aid kit; Allergy list; Auto-injector location; EpiPen steps; EpiPen administration; Post-EpiPen actions; Hospital visit; Emergency kit contents; School kit items
nodes_flat.content:
**1. Initial assessment and safety**

*   Immediately move the student away from the area where the wasps are present to prevent further stings.
*   Check for additional wasps nearby before intervening.

**2. Assess severity**

*   Ask and observe:
    *   Single sting or multiple stings?
    *   Are you known to be allergic to insect stings?
    *   Initial symptoms:
        *   Mild: pain, redness, localised swelling.
        *   Emergency: difficulty breathing, swelling of the face/neck, widespread hives, dizziness, vomiting.

**3. Immediate action according to the situation**

**A. Mild local reaction (most common)**

*   Remove the sting if present (wasps usually do not leave one, but check).
*   Wash the area with soap and water.
*   Apply a cold compress (ice wrapped in a cloth) for 10–15 minutes to reduce swelling and pain.
*   Elevate the affected area if possible (e.g. an arm).
*   Observe the student for at least 30 minutes to rule out delayed reactions.

**B. Severe reaction (anaphylaxis) – EMERGENCY**

Warning symptoms:

*   Swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat.
*   Shortness of breath or a feeling of tightness in the chest.
*   Severe dizziness or loss of consciousness.
*   Generalised urticaria.
*   Persistent vomiting.

**Action:**

*   Call 112 immediately (state: *“possible anaphylactic reaction due to sting”*).
*   Administer self-injectable adrenaline (if reported by the family and available at the school).
*   Place the child lying down with legs elevated (if conscious).
*   Do not leave the child unattended until medical assistance arrives.

**4. Information and monitoring**

*   Notify the family immediately.
*   Record the incident (time, place, symptoms, and actions taken).
*   Monitor the student for at least 1 hour, even in mild cases, as delayed reactions may occur.

**5. Prevention**

*   Check the premises before use.
*   Inform staff to act quickly but calmly.
*   Ensure a first aid kit with instant cold packs and basic materials is available.
*   Keep a list of students with known allergies and the location of their auto-injectors.

**6. Training of staff in the use of an EpiPen**

**How to use the EpiPen:**

1.  Remove the safety cap.
2.  Hold firmly and place against the outer thigh (it can be applied over clothing).
3.  Press until you hear a click, hold for 5–10 seconds.
4.  Massage the area gently.

*   After use: call 112 and notify the family.
*   Ensure the child is taken to hospital, even if symptoms improve.
*   School Emergency Kit:
    *   Instant cold packs (for mild stings).
    *   Oral antihistamine (only if prescribed for the student).
    *   Gauze, gloves, disinfectant.
    *   List of emergency telephone numbers (112, family contacts).
    *   List of students with allergies and the location of auto-injectors (if applicable).

nodes_flat.title: Valencian Community Health Safety Reference Documents
nodes_flat.topics: Decreto 253/2019; School safety guidelines; Emergency protocols; Ley 10/2014; Health promotion programmes; Disease prevention; Ley 1/1994; School health actions; Ley 31/1995; Occupational risk prevention; Risk management; Prevention Services Regulation; Employee safety duty; Staff accident liability; Civil law responsibility; Prudent parent standard
nodes_flat.content:
The main legislation covering health and safety in educational settings in the Valencian Community includes the Decreto 253/2019, de 29 de noviembre (DOGV), which regulates the organisation and functioning of public centres providing Early Childhood and Primary Education. This decree establishes guidelines for comprehensive safety within schools, covering aspects related to infrastructure, emergency measures, and protocols for action.

The Ley 10/2014, de 29 de diciembre, de Salud de la Comunitat Valenciana, specifies that the competent authorities, in collaboration with the educational and child protection departments, will develop educational and training programmes to promote healthy habits and prevent diseases in the school environment.

The Ley 1/1994, de 28 de marzo, de Salud Escolar, also establishes actions related to health in schools, with a strong emphasis on promoting health and preventing diseases among the student population.

In addition, the Ley 31/1995 (Occupational Risk Prevention Act) and the Reglamento de los Servicios de Prevención (Regulation of Prevention Services) apply to the educational environment, ensuring that risks are identified, managed, and mitigated. Employees also have a duty to ensure their own and others' health and safety. It is rare for school staff to face prosecution under criminal law regarding accidents involving children, as responsibility is generally considered under civil law, with a standard of care similar to that of a prudent parent.

nodes_flat.title: Weather Alert Safety Protocol
nodes_flat.topics: Safety objective; Protocol establishment; Green risk definition; Yellow risk definition; Orange risk definition; Red risk definition; Green level actions; Normal activity maintenance; Yellow level outdoor suspension; Yellow level premises inspection; Yellow level alert monitoring; Yellow level family updates; Orange level outdoor suspension; Orange level premises inspection; Orange level alert monitoring; Orange level family updates; Orange level access assessment; Orange level early closure; Orange level travel caution; Red level closure criteria; Red level transport coordination; Red level evacuation plan; Red level authority contact; Red level preventive closure; Red level safety communication
nodes_flat.content:
To guarantee the safety of students, teaching and non-teaching staff, and families in weather alert situations, by establishing an action protocol in line with the level of risk determined by the Generalitat Valenciana, AEMET and the competent authorities.

| Level   | Meaning                                                               |
| :------ | :-------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Green** | There is no meteorological risk.                                      |
| **Yellow**| There is some meteorological risk for certain specific activities, but not for the population in general. |
| **Orange**| There is a significant meteorological risk. Unusual weather phenomena with a certain degree of danger for normal activities. |
| **Red**   | The meteorological risk is extreme. Unusual weather phenomena of exceptional intensity, posing a very high risk for the population. |

**GREEN LEVEL: NO RISK**

*   No special measures are taken.
*   The normal activity of the centre is maintained.

**YELLOW LEVEL: MODERATE RISK**

*   Activities at the centre:
    *   All outdoor activities (physical education, outdoor play, excursions) are suspended.
    *   A visual inspection of the premises is carried out to detect possible risks (pipes, drains, loose structures, etc.).
*   Monitoring and communication:
    *   The alert is monitored regularly through official emergency channels.
    *   Families are informed of the measures taken.

**ORANGE LEVEL: SIGNIFICANT RISK**

*   Activities at the centre:
    *   All outdoor activities (physical education, outdoor play, excursions) are suspended.
    *   A visual inspection of the premises is carried out to detect possible risks (pipes, drains, loose structures, etc.).
*   Monitoring and communication:
    *   The alert is monitored regularly through official emergency channels.
    *   Families are informed of the measures taken.
*   Transport and accessibility:
    *   Liaise with the town council or Civil Protection to assess the condition of the access roads to the centre.
    *   Early closure of the centre will be considered if conditions require it.
    *   If staff or families live in areas affected by the alert, they are urged to take extreme care when travelling and to notify the school of possible delays.

**RED LEVEL: EXTREME RISK**

*   Closure criteria:
    *   The Management may determine the total or partial closure of the educational centre depending on the severity of the alert and in accordance with the indications of the competent authorities (town council and/or Civil Protection).
*   Transport, accessibility and evacuation:
    *   Coordination with those responsible for school transport to ensure safe management.
    *   If the alert is declared during school hours, the evacuation or confinement plan due to weather risk will be evaluated and activated if necessary.
*   Monitoring and communication:
    *   Continuous contact with the town council and/or Civil Protection.
    *   In the event of preventive closure, staff and families will be informed not to attend.
    *   If the alert is declared while the centre is in operation, the necessary measures will be taken and communicated to guarantee everyone’s safety.

nodes_flat.title: Emergency coordination with GVA, local authorities
nodes_flat.topics: Follow GVA, local instructions; Center closure decisions; Consult GVA, AEMET, Civil Protection; Adapt necessary measures; GVA emergency levels; Assess additional safety
nodes_flat.content:
*   The instructions of the GVA and local authorities will be followed regarding any decision on the closure of the centre.
*   The emergency website of the Generalitat Valenciana ([https://www.112cv.gva.es/es/1-1-2-on-line](https://www.112cv.gva.es/es/1-1-2-on-line)), the AEMET website and the indications of Civil Protection will be consulted to adapt the necessary measures.
*   In the event of an emergency declared by the GVA at level 0, 1 or 2, the school will assess additional measures to ensure safety.

nodes_flat.title: Emergency class suspension and continuity
nodes_flat.topics: Official closure order; Classes suspended; Inform families, staff; Coordinate transport, services; School closure decision; Communicate closure early; Early dismissal option; Confinement procedure; Orange/red alert response; Follow GVA, Civil Protection; Inform families, support; Online learning option
nodes_flat.content:
**If the Generalitat Valenciana (GVA) or the town council orders the closure:**

*   Classes must be suspended.
*   The school will inform families and staff about the suspension of activities.
*   Coordination will be carried out with school transport and auxiliary services.

**If there is no closure order, but an evident risk exists:**

*   The school may decide to close as a precautionary measure.
*   The decision must be communicated to families in advance.
*   If the alert is activated during the school day, dismissal may be brought forward or confinement applied if access to the outside is unsafe.

**If an orange or red alert is expected outside school hours:**

*   The indications of Civil Protection and the GVA will be followed to evaluate the situation.
*   If classes are suspended, families will be informed and supported regarding the possibility of offering online educational activities if circumstances allow.

nodes_flat.title: Adverse weather preventive measures
nodes_flat.topics: Generator set review; Pipe, gutter, drain maintenance; First aid kit verification; Electrical equipment shutdown
nodes_flat.content:
To minimise the risks associated with adverse weather conditions, the following preventive measures are carried out periodically:

*   Periodic review of the condition of generator sets.
*   Cleaning and maintenance of pipes, gutters and drains.
*   Verification of first aid kits and materials.
*   Switching off unnecessary electrical equipment during storms.

nodes_flat.title: Monitoring Emergency Alerts and Sources

nodes_flat.content: Alerts will be checked through the official emergency website: [https://www.112cv.gva.es/es/1-1-2-on-line](https://www.112cv.gva.es/es/1-1-2-on-line), the AEMET website, and other official sources.

This protocol will be updated in line with current regulations and the recommendations of meteorological and civil protection authorities.

nodes_flat.topics: Alert levels; DANA school attendance; Community safety priority
nodes_flat.content:
Under normal conditions, Situation 1 (pre-emergency) corresponds to a yellow alert and Situation 2 (emergency) to an orange alert. However, we wish to make a special recommendation for towns affected by DANA events.

If your town is in Situation 2 due to DANA, we recommend that you do not attend school, even if the weather alert has not yet reached orange level.

Our priority is the safety of the entire educational community, and we thank you for your cooperation and prudence in these cases.

nodes_flat.title: School food policy scope and values
nodes_flat.topics: Policy application; Student staff; Food provision; Meals snacks diet; Montessori philosophy; Food preparation; Cultural approach; Nutritious meals; Environmental values
nodes_flat.content: This policy applies to all students and staff at Imagine Montessori School, covering all aspects of food provision, including meals, snacks, and dietary considerations. In alignment with our Montessori philosophy, it encompasses the preparation, serving, and cultural approach to food, ensuring that all meals are nutritious and supportive of the environmental values upheld by the school.

nodes_flat.title: Food Policy Terminology Definitions
nodes_flat.topics: Nutritive food; Organic products; Seasonal products; Local food sourcing; Special diet accommodation
nodes_flat.content:
**Nutritive Food:** Food that is balanced, providing essential nutrients to support physical, cognitive, and emotional development.

**Organic Products:** Food grown without synthetic pesticides, fertilizers, or genetically modified organisms (GMO), in accordance with organic farming standards.

**Seasonal Products:** Ingredients that are grown or harvested at the peak of their natural season, ensuring freshness and minimal environmental impact.

**Km 0:** Food sourced locally, minimizing transportation costs and environmental footprint.

**Special Diets:** We follow any special diet due to health (gluten-free, specific food allergies, with a certificate from allergologists or gastroenterologists), or personal (vegetarian, vegan) reasons.

nodes_flat.title: Comprehensive Food Policy Principles
nodes_flat.topics: Daily nutritious meals; Meal schedule; Whole foods focus; Healthy food restrictions; Organic local sourcing; Sustainability commitment; Dietary accommodations; Medical dietary needs; Personal dietary preferences; Weekly vegetarian meals; Montessori Practical Life; Student meal participation; Life skills learning; Classroom shared meals; Social emotional eating; Cook preparation care; Nourishing body mind; Monthly feedback collection; Menu review process; Menu improvement areas; Nutritional specialist review
nodes_flat.content:
**Nutritional Balance:** We are committed to offering three nutritious meals daily: A portion snack in the morning, lunch, and snack in the afternoon, with a focus on whole, unprocessed foods. Our meals are free of fried products, low in salt, and free from added sugars.

**Sustainability and Environment:** In line with Montessori values and our commitment to environmental stewardship, ingredients used in school meals are organic, seasonal, and locally sourced (“km 0”). This not only supports the health of our students but also contributes to global sustainability efforts.

**Inclusive Diets:** We recognize and accommodate specific dietary needs. This includes strict adherence to medical requirements (e.g., gluten intolerance, allergies with a certificate from allergologists or gastroenterologists) and personal dietary preferences (e.g., vegetarian, vegan). A minimum of a day each week is dedicated to vegetarian meals for the entire school community.

**Cultural and Social Aspects of Eating:** Meal times are integral to the Montessori "Practical Life" curriculum. Students actively participate in setting the table, serving food, and cleaning up afterward, learning valuable skills such as responsibility, community, and respect for their environment. Meals are shared within classrooms to foster a sense of community, making food a social experience that is nutritionally and emotionally enriching.

**Preparation with Care:** Our meals are lovingly prepared by our cooks, who bring passion and dedication to cooking. Their goal is to nourish the body and mind of each student and staff member through wholesome, carefully prepared meals.

**Feedback Mechanisms**

We collect monthly feedback from the Imagine team to review the menu for the following month and address any areas for improvement. All our menus are reviewed by a nutritional specialist to ensure they meet the highest standards of health and well-being.

nodes_flat.title: Religious Fasting: School Policy and Support
nodes_flat.topics: Religious fasting support; Formal request required; Fasting age considerations; Quiet spaces provided; Respect for fasters; Health priority; Emergency care; Student food requests; Activity adjustments; Afternoon fatigue accommodation; PE modifications; Personal choice; No school monitoring
nodes_flat.content:
Imagine Montessori School acknowledges and respects the cultural and religious diversity within our community. In alignment with our commitment to inclusivity and student well-being, the following measures are in place to support students fasting for religious reasons:

*   **Formal request** - Families who wish for their child to participate in fasting at school must submit a request to the Secretary. This request will initiate a Phidias process that must be reviewed and signed.
*   **Fasting Age Considerations** – While fasting is a personal and family decision, it is generally recommended for older children who can manage the physical and mental demands. Fasting during school hours is generally considered appropriate from puberty age, between 12 and 15 years old. Younger children who wish to participate should do so in a flexible manner, with shorter fasts or partial fasting, in consultation with their families.
*   **Provision of Quiet Spaces** – Fasting students will have access to a quiet space, free from food smells, such as the science lab, where they may read, engage in quiet conversation, or pray as needed. However, as Imagine Montessori School follows a classroom-based dining model without a common dining hall, the availability of such a space will depend on logistical possibilities within the school. Every effort will be made to provide a suitable environment that respects the needs of fasting students while maintaining the integrity of our Montessori approach to shared meal experiences.
*   **Respect for Fasting Students** – Staff and students are encouraged to be mindful of those fasting for religious reasons by refraining from eating or drinking in their presence.
*   **Health and Well-being Considerations** – While fasting is a personal and family decision, there may be circumstances in which a student requires food or water during the school day, such as feeling unwell, experiencing dizziness, due to menstruation or other health reasons.
    In cases where a student is visibly unwell or at risk of dehydration, the school reserves the right to provide immediate care, including offering water or food, if necessary, before contacting the parents. The well-being and safety of the child will always be the priority.
    Furthermore, if a fasting student requests food and/or water, it will be made available to them according to the school's standard procedures.
*   **Outdoor and Physical Activity Adjustments** – Fasting students may use outdoor spaces for socializing but should be mindful of their own energy levels and avoid excessive exposure to heat or strenuous activity. Parents are encouraged to have conversations with their children about these considerations to help them make informed choices regarding their activities while fasting.
*   **Afternoon Accommodations** – As fasting students may experience fatigue later in the day, if needed, we encourage them to find a quiet space to rest, read, or engage in low-energy activities as needed.
*   **Physical Education (PE) Modifications** – Participation in PE may be physically demanding while fasting. Students who require adjustments should be permitted to sit out or engage in alternative low-intensity activities, under the supervision of the same teacher.
*   **Respect for Personal and Family Decisions** – While the school provides accommodations for students who are fasting for religious reasons, it is considered a personal and family choice. The school does not monitor or enforce fasting, and students are responsible for managing their own participation in accordance with their family’s expectations.

nodes_flat.topics: Valencian school regulation; Valencian health law; School health promotion; School health actions; Montessori principles alignment; WHO health definition
nodes_flat.content:
*   Decreto 253/2019, de 29 de noviembre: Regulates the organisation and functioning of public schools providing Early Childhood and Primary Education in the Valencian Community.
*   Ley 10/2014, de 29 de diciembre, de Salud de la Comunitat Valenciana: Article 56 specifies the importance of promoting healthy habits and preventing diseases in the school environment.
*   Ley 1/1994, de 28 de marzo, de Salud Escolar: Establishes actions related to health in schools, with a focus on promoting health and preventing diseases.

*   **World Health Organization Constitution**: (WHO): “Health is a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.”

nodes_flat.title: Key Data Protection Terminology
nodes_flat.topics: Personal Data; Processing; Data Controller; Consent; GDPR; LOPDGDD; Data Subject; Third Parties; Data Protection Officer; Sensitive Data; Data Breach; User Rights; International Data Transfer
nodes_flat.content:
**Personal Data**: Any information relating to an identified or identifiable individual. This includes but is not limited to identifying information such as name, address, contact details, and any other data that may be collected through our services, such as social circumstances, employment status, or banking information.

**Processing**: Any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, storage, organization, retrieval, use, dissemination, or destruction. This term refers to how your personal data is managed throughout its lifecycle.

**Data Controller**: The entity responsible for determining the purposes and means of processing personal data. In this case, the data controller is ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L. (Imagine Montessori School), part of ZUBI GROUP IMPACT, S.L.

**Consent**: A clear, informed, and explicit agreement from the individual to process their personal data. Consent can be given electronically or on paper, as specified in our data collection forms. Consent is required for any processing activity, and it can be withdrawn at any time.

**GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)**: The regulation (EU) 2016/679, which governs data protection and privacy across the European Union and aims to protect the privacy and personal data of individuals.

**LOPDGDD (Ley Orgánica 3/2018, de 5 de diciembre)**: The Spanish Organic Law on Data Protection and the Guarantee of Digital Rights, which complements the GDPR and governs data protection in Spain.

**Data Subject**: The individual whose personal data is being processed. In this policy, the data subject refers to individuals who provide their personal data to ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L., either as users or clients.

**Third Parties**: Any organization or individual outside of ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L. to whom personal data may be disclosed for processing, in compliance with legal obligations or contractual agreements.

**Data Protection Officer (DPO)**: The individual responsible for overseeing data protection activities within the entity. This includes ensuring compliance with data protection laws and addressing any concerns about personal data processing.

**Sensitive Data**: Personal data that is more sensitive in nature, such as data about race, ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, or health information. We do not collect sensitive data unless required by law.

**Data Breach**: Any unauthorized access to or disclosure of personal data, accidental or unlawful destruction, or loss of personal data. We implement strict security measures to prevent such incidents.

**User Rights**: The rights granted to individuals under data protection laws, including the right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, and object to the processing of their personal data. Users may also request data portability or withdraw consent for specific processing activities.

**International Data Transfer**: The movement of personal data outside the European Economic Area (EEA). Personal data will only be transferred to countries with adequate protection or under legal safeguards such as standard contractual clauses.

nodes_flat.title: Data Protection Policy Principles
nodes_flat.topics: Legality; Legitimate purposes; GDPR consent; Contractual measures; Data minimisation; Purpose limitation; Data accuracy; Data security; Technical measures; Organisational measures; Data retention; International data transfers; EU/EEA data protection; Third-party data access; Contractual safeguards; Direct marketing; Cookie legislation
nodes_flat.content:
We have always been committed to delivering our services to the highest standards, which includes treating your data securely and transparently. Our principles are:

**Legality:** We will only collect your personal data for specified explicit and legitimate purposes. To comply with our legal and regulatory obligations, we rely on the following provisions:

*   Art. 6.1. GDPR. On the express consent you have given us for the processing of your data.
*   Art. 6.2 GDPR. For the performance of a contract to which you as a data subject are a party or for the implementation of contractual measures at your request.

**Data minimisation:** We limit the collection of personal data to what is strictly relevant and necessary for the purposes for which it was collected.

**Purpose limitation**: We will only collect your personal data for the stated purposes and only in accordance with your wishes.

**Accuracy**: We will keep your personal data accurate and up to date.

**Data Security:** We implement appropriate technical and organisational measures proportionate to the risks to ensure that your data is not subject to damage, such as unauthorised disclosure or access, accidental or unlawful destruction or accidental loss or alteration and any other form of unlawful processing.

**Retention:** We retain your personal data lawfully and appropriately and only for as long as is necessary for the purposes for which it was collected.

International transfers: Where your data is to be transferred outside the EU/EEA it will be adequately protected.

**Third parties**: Access and transfer of personal data to third parties is carried out in accordance with applicable laws and regulations and with appropriate contractual safeguards.

**Direct Marketing and Cookies:** We comply with applicable advertising and cookie legislation.

nodes_flat.title: Data Ownership and Processing Purpose
nodes_flat.topics: Data provision; Interaction data; Express consent; Electronic consent; No processing without consent; Consent record; Written consent; Positive act consent; Service access data; Contact forms
nodes_flat.content:
When you use the various electronic and paper registration forms in the entity you provide us with personal data that are incorporated into our database. You also generate information because of your interaction with us that we may retain. All data processing carried out by the entity is always and, in any case, based on your express consent.

To obtain consent, by electronic means, you must tick the checkboxes accompanying the data collection form. Without express authorisation, the data will not be processed, and the website will not allow the process to continue when processing is necessary. Our website generates a record of each of the consents given to be able to accredit their effective provision and date.

When we collect information from the user by another means, for example, a written form, we will obtain the user's consent to the processing by means of a signature on a paper document.

Express consent is understood to be that which implies a positive act on the part of the user, never basing processing on the mere silence or inaction of the user.

Likewise, the data you provide us with to access any service or functionality that we make available to you on the website will also be processed, always informing you of the processing that will be carried out and obtaining your express consent.

In addition, we make available to users of the website, forms to contact us, so you can send us your suggestions.

nodes_flat.title: Exercising your data protection rights
nodes_flat.topics: Access right; Rectification right; Erasure right; Restriction right; Opposition right; Direct marketing objection; Data portability right; Withdraw consent; Exercise rights procedure; Contact information; Identity verification; Lodge complaint; Supervisory authority
nodes_flat.content:
In accordance with the applicable regulation, you have the following rights:

*   **Access**: you can obtain information regarding the processing of your personal data and a copy of it.
*   **Rectification**: if you consider your personal data to be incorrect or incomplete, you may request that such data be amended accordingly.
*   **Erasure**: you may request the erasure of your personal data to the extent permitted by law.
*   **Restriction**: you may request the restriction of the processing of your personal data.
*   **Opposition**: you can object to the processing of your data considering your particular situation. You have an absolute right to object to the processing of your personal data for direct marketing purposes, which includes profiling related to direct marketing.
*   **Portability of your data**: where applicable, you have the right to have your data returned to you or, where possible, transferred to a third party.

Where you have given your consent for a specific purpose, you have the right to withdraw your consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of the processing.

If you wish to exercise the aforementioned rights, please send a letter or e-mail to the following addresses: C/ Meliana, 5, CP 46019 Valencia, or to the email dpd@imginemontessori.es including a copy or scanned copy of your NIF or supporting document in order to verify your identity.

In accordance with the applicable legislation, in addition to the aforementioned rights, you have the right to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority, the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD), located at Calle Jorge Juan, 6 CP 28001 Madrid, if we have not previously responded to your request to exercise your rights within a period of one month.

nodes_flat.title: Data Security Measures and Risk Analysis
nodes_flat.topics: Consent for offline data; Data processing risk analysis; Risk minimization; User rights protection; Strictest security implementation; Data integrity preservation; Third-party security measures; Unauthorized access prevention; Supplier selection criteria; Legal security compliance
nodes_flat.content:
When we collect information from the user by another means, for example, a written form, we will obtain the user's consent to the processing by means of a signature on a paper document.

The entity has carried out an exhaustive risk analysis of all the data processing carried out. As a result of this analysis, it has defined each process and tried to minimise any possible risks, guaranteeing with the utmost diligence the adequate protection of the Fundamental Rights of users within the entity's environment.

The entity is aware of the user's concern for security and has therefore chosen to implement the strictest levels of security that allow the proper functioning of the website, its functionalities and its services, preserving the integrity of the data and information stored. The services are provided with its own technological resources and sometimes by third parties who have the necessary security measures to ensure the impossibility of unauthorised access according to the current ordinary technique and technology. At Imagine Montessori School we want the service that our clients receive to offer a satisfactory and secure user experience, which is why we only choose suppliers with a reputable track record and technical solvency that allow compliance with current legal security regulations, to prevent loss, misuse or access by unauthorised persons.

nodes_flat.title: Policy legal context and updates
nodes_flat.topics: Policy supplements law; Law prevails; Policy periodic review; Policy amendment; Notify policy changes; Confirm policy acceptance
nodes_flat.content:
The requirements of this Policy supplement, and do not replace, any other requirements existing under applicable data protection law, which shall prevail in any event.

This Policy is subject to periodic review and may be amended by us at any time. When this happens, we will notify you of any changes and ask you to re-read the most recent version of our Policy and confirm your acceptance of it.

nodes_flat.title: Whistleblowing Channel Policy Scope
nodes_flat.topics: Policy scope; Whistleblowing channel users; Reporting obligation; No retaliation; Confidentiality; Channel purpose; Proper channel use
nodes_flat.content:
This policy is intended for employees, clients, suppliers, and third parties involved in the Company's operations, who may use the Whistleblowing Channel as detailed herein.

All employees (regardless of the legal form of their employment or service relationship), suppliers, sponsors, collaborators, agents, and members of the Company's various collegiate bodies are required to report any irregularity or breach within their objective scope through the Whistleblowing Channel, without fear of dismissal or other forms of retaliation. The information provided will be treated with utmost confidentiality.

The Whistleblowing Channel is intended as an instrument for reporting irregularities or breaches. Therefore, it should not be used indiscriminately but solely for the purposes for which it is designed.

nodes_flat.title: Whistleblowing Policy Key Terminology
nodes_flat.topics: Whistleblower definition; Whistleblowing Channel definition; Report definition; System Administrator role; Investigation Committee role; Informant definition; Affected Individual definition; Ad Hoc Team purpose
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Whistleblower**: An individual who reports, or provides information concerning, a potential breach, offence, or irregularity in the operations of the Company, including employees, clients, suppliers, partners, or any other relevant third parties.
*   **Whistleblowing Channel**: Our platform and communication system made available in our website to all individuals connected to our School, allowing for the confidential and secure reporting of any breach or irregularity as defined in this policy.
*   **Report**: Any communication made through the Whistleblowing Channel, providing information about a potential breach, offence, or irregularity that may require investigation.
*   **System Administrator**: The individual responsible for the operation and management of the Whistleblowing Channel, including the receipt, processing, and registration of reports.
*   **Investigation Committee**: A designated group responsible for overseeing the investigation of the reported facts, ensuring compliance with the internal procedures and regulations.
*   **Informant**: The person submitting a report through the Whistleblowing Channel.
*   **Affected Individual**: Any person or entity that is implicated or involved in a report submitted through the Whistleblowing Channel.
*   **Ad Hoc Team**: A specific group designated to handle particular reports, based on the subject matter or potential conflict of interest of the System Administrator.

nodes_flat.title: Whistleblowing Channel Policy Principles
nodes_flat.topics: Policy principles; Regulatory compliance; EU Directive; Whistleblower protection; Channel objective; Eligible reporters; Reportable incidents; Internal procedure violations; AML breaches; Data protection breaches; Market abuse breaches; Sexual freedom breaches; Equality breaches; Code of Conduct breaches; Channel accessibility; Corporate website; Reportable risks; Secure communication; Privacy; Confidentiality; Anonymous reporting
nodes_flat.content:
This policy has been developed by Zubi Education, SL (hereinafter referred to as "the Company"), to maintain group-wide consistency of action. Its objective is to outline the procedure followed within the internal whistleblowing channel, in compliance with various regulatory provisions, including Article 8 of Directive (EU) 2019/1937 of the European Parliament and Council dated 23 October 2019, regarding the protection of individuals who report breaches of EU law.

The objective of the Whistleblowing Channel is to enable employees, clients, suppliers, and third parties associated with the Company or its operations to report any incident within the Company that could represent a breach, offence, or violation of our internal procedures, among others:

*   Breach of Anti-Money Laundering (AML) regulations
*   Breach of Data Protection regulations
*   Breach of Market Abuse regulations
*   Breach of comprehensive sexual freedom assurance regulations
*   Breach of regulations ensuring effective equality between men and women
*   Internal Code of Conduct

The Company’s Whistleblowing Channel is accessible through the corporate website: , and is available to individuals aware of potential administrative and/or serious criminal risks attributable to any person or entity linked to the group in their professional capacity.

This Channel enables secure communication, ensuring that privacy and confidentiality are maintained throughout the process. Reports may be submitted anonymously if the whistleblower so chooses.

nodes_flat.title: Whistleblowing Channel General Responsibilities
nodes_flat.topics: Policy approval; Resource provision; Policy updating; Channel operation; Whistleblower confidentiality; Complaint recording; Documentation preservation; Information provision; AML compliance support; Code of Conduct support; Market Abuse support; Data protection support; Equality regulations support
nodes_flat.content:
The general responsibilities regarding the Whistleblowing Channel are outlined below:

**Board of Directors:**

*   Approval of this Policy and its subsequent updates.
*   Provision of the necessary resources for the proper functioning of the Channel.

**Control Unit:**

*   Updating of this policy.

**System Administrator:**

*   Operation of the whistleblowing channel.
*   Ensuring confidentiality of whistleblowers, when applicable.
*   Recording complaints.
*   Preservation of documentation.
*   Providing information to the investigative committee in cases of complaints of workplace and/or sexual harassment.

**Internal Control Body for Anti-Money Laundering (AML) Compliance:**

*   Support for the Criminal Risk Prevention Body in complaints related to breaches of AML/CTF law.

**Internal Code of Conduct Monitoring Body:**

*   Support for the Criminal Risk Prevention Body in complaints regarding breaches of the Company's Internal Code of Conduct.
*   Support for the Criminal Risk Prevention Body in complaints related to breaches of Market Abuse regulations.

**Data Protection Security Officer:**

*   Support for the Criminal Risk Prevention Body in complaints related to breaches of data protection regulations.

**Equal Opportunities Officer:**

*   Support for the Body responsible for compliance with equality regulations between men and women, and for ensuring comprehensive sexual freedom.

nodes_flat.title: Whistleblower Channel Communication Guidelines
nodes_flat.topics: Preferred reporting method; Confidential data processing; Whistleblower channel accessibility; Who can report; Reporting irregularities; Presumption of innocence; Investigation procedures; Investigation response time; Response time extension; Informant communication; Requesting additional information; Referral to Public Prosecutor; Referral to European Prosecutor
nodes_flat.content:
The public whistleblowing channel (“canal de denuncias”) on our website should be used as the preferred method for submitting information, as it ensures confidential data processing in accordance with the Data Protection Act.

The "Whistleblower Channel" is available on our website and is accessible to all individuals who have a relationship with us, including clients, employees, suppliers, partners, or any other parties connected to our activities. This channel allows the reporting of irregularities or inappropriate behavior, ensuring confidentiality and the right to the presumption of innocence for all involved parties. The information received through this channel will be handled with the utmost seriousness and in compliance with personal data protection regulations, ensuring that investigation procedures are conducted in accordance with applicable laws.

As a general rule, the maximum time to respond to investigation actions shall not exceed three months from the receipt of the report or, if no acknowledgment of receipt was sent to the informant, three months from the expiration of the seven-day period following the submission of the report, except in cases of special complexity that require an extension of the period, in which case it may be extended by a maximum of an additional three months.

At various stages of the processing procedure, there is provision for maintaining communication with the informant and, if deemed necessary, requesting additional information from the informant, unless they have waived this option.

In all cases, the right to the presumption of innocence and the honour of the affected parties shall be respected, as well as the provisions on the protection of personal data as stipulated.

If the reported facts are potentially indicative of a crime, the information will be referred immediately to the Public Prosecutor’s Office. Should the facts affect the financial interests of the European Union, they will be referred to the European Public Prosecutor’s Office.

nodes_flat.title: Report Receipt and Registration Process
nodes_flat.topics: Anonymous reporting; Informant confidentiality; Notification preferences; Decline communications; Transcript review; Transcript correction; Case recording; Identification code; Secure database storage; Registered details; Acknowledgment within 7 days; Acknowledgment exceptions; Confidentiality jeopardized
nodes_flat.content:
Information can be provided anonymously. In other cases, the identity of the informant will be kept confidential.

When submitting information, the informant may provide an address, email, or secure location for receiving notifications and may also expressly decline to receive any communications regarding actions taken by the Company because of the report.

Without prejudice to the rights accorded to them under data protection regulations, the informant will be given the opportunity to review, correct, and sign the transcript of the message.

Each case will be recorded within the whistleblowing channel and assigned an identification code. All information recorded within the whistleblowing channel will be stored in a secure, restricted-access database, where the following details of each communication will be registered:

a)  Date of receipt.
b)  Identification code.
c)  Actions taken.
d)  Measures adopted.
e)  Closure date.

Upon receipt of the information, an acknowledgment will be issued within a maximum period of 7 calendar days from receipt, unless the informant has expressly declined to receive communications related to the investigation, or unless doing so could jeopardise the confidentiality of the report.

nodes_flat.title: Whistleblowing Report Admission Procedure
nodes_flat.topics: Report verification; Scope check; Report reassignment; Conflict of interest; Ad hoc team; Team composition; Impartiality; Decision timeframe; Report dismissal criteria; Implausible facts; No legal breach; Unfounded report; Crime indication; Prosecutor referral; No new information; Dismissal communication; Report admission; Admission communication; Informant notification waiver; Direct prosecutor referral; Competent authority referral
nodes_flat.content:
Once the information is registered, the System Administrator will verify whether it presents facts or behaviours that fall within the scope of Annex I. They will also determine if the report needs to be reassigned to another person or management team due to any of the following circumstances:

1.  Conflict of interest of the System Administrator, as they may be personally affected by the report.
2.  Existence of an ad hoc team responsible for managing the report based on the subject matter.

The ad hoc team for handling reports will, in this case, be composed of the Chief Officer People of Zubi Group, ensuring impartiality in the process.

Following this preliminary analysis, a decision will be made within a maximum period of ten business days from the registration date of the information:

a)  **Dismiss the report** in any of the following cases:
    1.  When the facts reported are entirely implausible.
    2.  When the facts reported do not constitute a breach of the legal framework covered under Annex I.
    3.  When the report is manifestly unfounded, or there are, in the System Administrator’s opinion, reasonable indications that it was obtained through the commission of a crime. In this latter case, in addition to dismissal, a detailed report of the facts deemed to constitute a crime will be forwarded to the Public Prosecutor’s Office.
    4.  When the report contains no new and significant information regarding breaches in comparison with a previous report for which the procedures have been concluded, unless there are new factual or legal circumstances that justify a different follow-up. In such cases, the System Administrator will issue a reasoned notification of the decision.
    The dismissal will be communicated to the informant within five business days.

b)  **Admit the report for processing**.
    The admission of the report will be communicated to the informant within seven business days, unless the informant has waived the right to receive communications from the Company’s System Administrator.

c)  **Immediately forward the information to the Public Prosecutor’s Office** if the facts may indicate a crime, or to the European Public Prosecutor’s Office if the facts affect the financial interests of the European Union.

d)  **Refer the report** to the competent authority, entity, or body for processing.

nodes_flat.title: Whistleblowing Investigation Process and Rights
nodes_flat.topics: Investigation scope; Fact verification; Affected person notification; Summarized facts disclosure; Right to submit arguments; Personal data handling; Delayed disclosure conditions; Evidence concealment prevention; Informant identity protection; No report access; Respondent summary provision; Affected individual interview; Presenting evidence; Presumption of innocence; Right to defense; Case file access; Informant identity redaction; Heard at any stage; Administrator confidentiality; Personnel cooperation; Information provision obligation; Personal data requests
nodes_flat.content:
The investigation will encompass all actions aimed at verifying the credibility of the reported facts.

It will be ensured that the person affected by the information is informed of the report, as well as of the facts presented in a summarised form. Additionally, they will be informed of their right to submit written arguments and the handling of their personal data. However, this information may be provided at the hearing stage if prior disclosure could facilitate the concealment, destruction, or alteration of evidence.

In no case will the identity of the informant be disclosed to the affected parties, nor will they be given access to the report. During the investigation, a summary of the reported facts will be provided to the respondent. This information may also be presented at the hearing if prior disclosure could facilitate the concealment, destruction, or alteration of evidence.

Without prejudice to the right to submit written arguments, the investigation will include, whenever possible, an interview with the affected individual in which they are invited to present their version of the events and to provide any evidence they consider appropriate and relevant, all while respecting the presumption of innocence.

To ensure the right to a defence, the affected individual will have access to the case file without revealing any information that could identify the informant, and they may be heard at any point in the proceedings.

The System Administrator is obligated to maintain confidentiality regarding the information they acquire during this investigation.

All Company personnel are required to cooperate with the investigation and must comply with any requests made to provide documentation, data, or any information related to the ongoing procedures, including personal data if required.

nodes_flat.title: Proceedings Conclusion Decisions and Data Retention
nodes_flat.topics: Investigation decisions; Case filing; Prosecutor referral; Authority transfer; Disciplinary proceedings; Whistleblowing record content; Record access; Data retention; Employee sanctions; Company judicial referral; Third-party sanctions
nodes_flat.content:
At the conclusion of the investigation, the System Administrator will adopt one of the following decisions:

a)  **Filing of the case**, which will be notified to the informant and, if applicable, to the affected individual. In such cases, the informant will retain the right to protection as provided by this law, unless, because of the actions carried out during the investigation phase, it is concluded that the report should have been dismissed based on one of the grounds specified in this Procedure.
b)  **Referral to the Public Prosecutor’s Office** if, despite initially finding no indication that the facts may constitute a crime, this determination changes over the course of the investigation. If the crime affects the financial interests of the European Union, the case will be referred to the European Public Prosecutor’s Office.
c)  **Transfer of all actions to the competent authority**, in accordance with the provisions of this Procedure.
d)  **Initiation of disciplinary proceedings**.

Upon conclusion of all actions, the System Administrator will retain information related to the report within the whistleblowing channel software, which will contain:

a)  Date of receipt.
b)  Classification of the report.
c)  Closure date.
d)  Actions taken.

This record will not be public, and only upon a reasoned request from the competent judicial authority, within the framework of a judicial proceeding and under its supervision, may partial or full access to the content of the record be granted.

Personal data related to received reports and internal investigations referred to in the preceding section will only be retained for as long as necessary and proportionate to comply with applicable legislation. In no case will data be retained for more than ten years.

If the investigation concludes with verified proof that the reported facts are true and involve irregular or unlawful conduct, the accused may be subject to sanctions in accordance with the applicable Collective Bargaining Agreement, labour legislation, and other civil and commercial obligations held by Company staff, executive personnel, or the Board of Directors.

The Company may also refer the matter to the relevant judicial authorities if deemed necessary.

If the accused is a third party with no employment relationship with the Company (supplier, commercial agent, or business partner), applicable sanctions will be limited to the commercial sphere (limitation of actions, unilateral termination of the contract by the Company), without prejudice to the aforementioned communication to the competent judicial authorities.

nodes_flat.title: False or Malicious Claims Reporting Policy
nodes_flat.topics: Whistleblowing channel responsibility; False claims consequences; Disciplinary action; Commercial measures; Malicious report handling; HR disciplinary referral; Sanction proposal; Disciplinary determination
nodes_flat.content:
The Company’s whistleblowing channel must be used responsibly and appropriately. Reporting false facts with a malicious and morally dishonest attitude constitutes a breach of the good faith that should govern professional and employment relationships within the Company and may lead to disciplinary action in accordance with the applicable Collective Bargaining Agreement or, for other stakeholders, commercial measures.

If, after proper analysis, it is concluded that the reported facts are manifestly false and that the report was submitted with malicious intent and in bad faith: (i) the report will be filed, (ii) the matter will be referred to the Human Resources Department to propose disciplinary measures in accordance with the applicable Collective Bargaining Agreement, and (iii) a written sanction proposal will be submitted to the General Human Resources Directorate or, where applicable, to the Board of Directors, which will determine the disciplinary action to be applied to the malicious informant.

nodes_flat.title: Whistleblowing Report Personal Data Protection
nodes_flat.topics: Organic Law 3/2018; AML/CTF Law 10/2010; Subject processing time; Informant confidentiality; Subject rights limitation; Informant data exclusion; Confidentiality prevails Law 2/2023; Privacy policy link
nodes_flat.content:
The processing of personal data arising from the application of this Procedure will be governed by the provisions of Article 11 of Organic Law 3/2018 of 5 December, on Personal Data Protection and the Guarantee of Digital Rights, unless the report is related to matters of anti-money laundering, counter-terrorist financing, and/or sanctions, in which case the provisions of Law 10/2010 of 28 April and its implementing regulations on anti-money laundering, counter-terrorist financing, and specific sanctions will apply. Lastly, the person who is the subject of the report will be informed of the estimated time for processing the report.

In any case, the Company, as a guarantee of confidentiality for the informant, confirms that, generally, the exercise of rights by the person under investigation may be limited due to the specific nature of the report and may only be exercised regarding their own personal data that is being processed. Under no circumstances will the rights exercised by the person under investigation include data relating to the informant. If it is not possible to adopt measures to safeguard the informant’s confidentiality when notifying the person under investigation, the informant's right to confidentiality will prevail in accordance with Article 31.2 of Law 2/2023 of 20 February, which regulates the protection of individuals who report regulatory breaches and fights against corruption.

Further information is available in our privacy policy at [https://imaginemontessori.es/politica-de-privacidad/](https://imaginemontessori.es/politica-de-privacidad/).

nodes_flat.content: | Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing and Sanctions | Money laundering is defined as: i) Conversion or transfer of assets, knowing they derive from criminal activity or involvement in such activity, intending to conceal the illicit origin of the assets or to help individuals involved evade legal consequences; ii) Concealing or disguising the nature, origin, location, disposition, movement, or ownership of assets or rights to assets, knowing that these derive from criminal activity; iii) Acquisition, possession, or use of assets, knowing, at the time of receipt, that they derive from criminal activity or participation therein; iv) Participation in any of the above activities, association for such acts, attempts to commit them, or aiding, instigating, or advising others in carrying them out. |

nodes_flat.topics: EU Whistleblower Protection Directive; Anti-Money Laundering Law; Personal Data Protection Law; Spanish Whistleblower Protection; Collective Investment Institutions; Spanish Penal Code; Securities Market Act; GDPR; Sexual Freedom Assurance Law; Gender Equality Law; Right to Privacy; Spanish Constitution; Worker Privacy Rights; Harassment Protection; Harassment Definition; WHO Health Definition; Occupational Risk Protection; Preventive Action Principles; LGBT+ Rights Law; Trans Equality Law
nodes_flat.content:
*   **DIRECTIVE (EU) 2019/1937 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL** of 23 October 2019 on the protection of persons who report breaches of Union law.
*   **Law 10/2010, of 28 April on Anti-Money Laundering**.
*   **Organic Law 3/2018, of 5 December** on the Protection of Personal Data.
*   **Law 2/2023, of 20 February on Whistleblower Protection**.
*   **Law 35/2003, of 4 November, on Collective Investment Institutions**.
*   **Organic Law 10/1995, of 23 November, the Penal Code**.
*   **Royal Legislative Decree 4/2015, of 23 October**, approving the revised text of the Securities Market Act.
*   **REGULATION (EU) 2016/679 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL** of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons concerning personal data processing and on the free movement of such data (General Data Protection Regulation).
*   **Organic Law 10/2022, of 6 September on comprehensive sexual freedom assurance**.
*   **Organic Law 3/2007, of 22 March for effective equality between men and women**.
*   **Article 18.1 of the Spanish Constitution**: “The right to honour, personal and family privacy, and one’s own image is guaranteed.”
*   **Article 4.2.e) of the Workers’ Statute**: “Workers are entitled to respect for their privacy and consideration of their dignity, including protection from verbal and physical harassment of a sexual nature and harassment based on racial or ethnic origin, religion or beliefs, disability, age, or sexual orientation.”
*   **European Parliament, Article 2 of Directive 2002/73/C of 23 September 2003**, and **Law 63/2003, of 30 December**, on fiscal, administrative, and social order measures, Article 28.2, which introduce the concept of harassment. This law defines harassment as “any unwanted conduct related to racial or ethnic origin, religion or beliefs, disabilities, age, or sexual orientation of a person, aimed at or resulting in violating dignity and creating an intimidating, humiliating, or offensive environment.”
*   **World Health Organization Constitution**: (WHO): “Health is a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.”
*   **Art. 14 of the Occupational Health and Safety Law, right to protection from occupational risks**: “Workers have the right to effective protection in matters of safety and health at work.”
*   **Art. 15 of the Prevention Law, principles of preventive action**: “The employer will apply measures that integrate the general duty of Prevention.”
*   **Law 4/2023 for real and effective equality of trans individuals and for the guarantee of LGBT+ rights**.

selected_nodes.title: Staff absences evacuation duties
selected_nodes.content_preview: Should any member of staff be absent, their duties in evacuating the children in their care from the building will be undertaken by the teacher or adult in charge of the class. Supply staff will also be required to sweep any areas they pass through for personnel as they exit the building.

step2.sections_text:
[Section 1.5: Staff absences evacuation duties]
Should any member of staff be absent, their duties in evacuating the children in their care from the building will be undertaken by the teacher or adult in charge of the class. Supply staff will also be required to sweep any areas they pass through for personnel as they exit the building.
step2.raw_response: Yes, supply teachers have an additional duty to sweep any areas they pass through for personnel as they exit the building [1.5].
step2.answer: Yes, supply teachers have an additional duty to sweep any areas they pass through for personnel as they exit the building [1.5].

queries.query_note: Title 'Staff absences evacuation duties' sounds unrelated to supply teachers. The one relevant sentence is 'Supply staff will also be required to sweep any areas they pass through.' If the model dismisses §1.5 as an 'absences' section, it misses the only supply-specific instruction.
